clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to his GP with a 2-year history of thickened, yellowish, crumbling toenails affecting all toenails on both feet. He has not sought treatment because he assumed it was cosmetic. He now reports a painful ulcer on the plantar aspect of his right great toe. On examination, the nail plate is markedly thickened and onychogryphotic, and there is surrounding periungual erythema and a 1.5 cm neuropathic ulcer beneath the distal edge of the nail. HbA1c is 9.2%. Which of the following is the most important reason to treat his onychomycosis aggressively in this context?
options: To improve cosmetic appearance and patient self-esteem
To prevent drug-drug interactions with his antidiabetic medications
Because the thickened nail acts as a mechanical trauma source perpetuating the diabetic foot ulcer
To eradicate dermatophytes that directly invade deeper subcutaneous tissues in diabetics
To reduce the risk of systemic terbinafine toxicity
explanation: In diabetic patients, grossly thickened onychogryphotic nails exert direct mechanical pressure on adjacent skin, creating or perpetuating neuropathic ulcers. Adequate nail debridement and antifungal treatment reduce this mechanical trauma, which is critical for wound healing. Dermatophytes themselves rarely invade beyond the nail unit even in diabetics; the danger is indirect through mechanical and microvascular mechanisms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cosmesis is a valid patient concern but is never the 'most important' reason in a patient with an active foot ulcer and poor glycaemic control.
Terbinafine has few clinically relevant interactions with standard antidiabetic agents; this is not the key concern here.
This is the correct answer — the thickened nail mechanically perpetuates the ulcer beneath it.
Dermatophytes do not typically invade subcutaneous tissues; secondary bacterial infection is the real invasive risk, not the dermatophyte itself.
Systemic terbinafine toxicity (hepatotoxicity) is a monitoring concern but not the primary reason to treat in this scenario.
clinicalPearl: Onychogryphosis in a diabetic foot is a mechanical hazard — nail debridement is part of wound care, not just cosmetics.
keyClue: Thickened nail with plantar ulcer directly beneath its distal edge in a poorly controlled diabetic.
commonExamTrap: Candidates focus on antifungal drug choice rather than the mechanical complication driving the ulcer.
missedConcept: Nail plate thickening as a physical cause of pressure injury in the neuropathic foot.

topic: Erythema multiforme major — Stevens-Johnson syndrome progression
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever (39.4°C), painful oral erosions, bilateral conjunctival injection with purulent discharge, and a widespread rash. The rash consists of targetoid lesions on the trunk and extremities with central blistering, and there is detachment of the epidermis involving approximately 18% of body surface area. She was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 10 days ago for a UTI. Which of the following prognostic tools and its associated finding most accurately predicts her in-hospital mortality?
options: SCORAD score >50, predicting 30% mortality
SCORTEN score ≥3, predicting approximately 35% mortality
ABSI score >8, predicting 60% mortality
DLQI score >20, predicting significant morbidity but not mortality
Child-Pugh score B, predicting hepatic involvement and 20% mortality
explanation: SCORTEN (SCORe of Toxic Epidermal Necrosis) is the validated prognostic scoring system for SJS/TEN, incorporating age, malignancy, heart rate, BSA detachment, serum urea, glucose, and bicarbonate. A SCORTEN of 3 carries approximately 35% predicted mortality. This patient has multiple risk factors (fever, widespread detachment >10% BSA indicates TEN overlap/TEN, age, likely metabolic derangement) that would contribute to an elevated SCORTEN.
wrongAnswerExplanations: SCORAD is used to assess severity of atopic dermatitis, not SJS/TEN, and does not predict mortality in this condition.
Correct — SCORTEN ≥3 corresponds to approximately 35% in-hospital mortality in TEN.
ABSI (Abbreviated Burn Severity Index) applies to thermal burns, not drug-induced epidermal necrolysis.
DLQI (Dermatology Life Quality Index) measures quality of life and has no mortality-predictive role.
Child-Pugh score assesses chronic liver disease severity and is not applicable here.
clinicalPearl: SCORTEN calculated within 24 hours of admission and again at day 3 is the standard prognostic tool for SJS/TEN — every clinician managing suspected drug reactions must know it.
keyClue: Epidermal detachment >10% BSA after sulfonamide exposure = TEN/SJS-TEN overlap; the prognostic question demands SCORTEN.
commonExamTrap: Confusing SCORAD (atopic eczema) with SCORTEN (TEN) — the names sound similar.
missedConcept: SCORTEN as the disease-specific mortality predictor for toxic epidermal necrolysis.
redFlagReason: Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN with >10% BSA detachment carries up to 35-50% mortality; immediate withdrawal of culprit drug, ICU/burns unit transfer, and supportive care are life-saving priorities.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 1-year history of progressive depigmented patches on her hands, perioral area, and around her eyes. Wood's lamp examination shows chalk-white fluorescence, confirming vitiligo. She has no other symptoms. Her GP initiates treatment with topical tacrolimus. During follow-up planning, which of the following associated conditions should be actively screened for at every annual review in patients with generalised vitiligo?
options: Coeliac disease and inflammatory bowel disease
Autoimmune thyroid disease, type 1 diabetes mellitus, and Addison's disease
Systemic lupus erythematosus and antiphospholipid syndrome
Pernicious anaemia and rheumatoid arthritis only
Psoriasis and alopecia areata only, as these are the only proven associations
explanation: Vitiligo is associated with a spectrum of organ-specific autoimmune diseases. The most clinically important and well-evidenced are autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease), type 1 diabetes mellitus, and Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency). Annual screening with TFTs, fasting glucose/HbA1c, and clinical assessment for adrenal insufficiency is recommended in guidelines for patients with generalised vitiligo.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Coeliac disease has weaker association with vitiligo and is not in the core annual screening panel; IBD is not a recognised major association.
Correct — autoimmune thyroid disease, T1DM, and Addison's disease are the primary screening targets.
SLE and antiphospholipid syndrome are not in the standard vitiligo surveillance protocol.
Pernicious anaemia is a valid association but excluding thyroid disease and T1DM from the list makes this option incomplete and therefore wrong.
Psoriasis is not causally linked to vitiligo; alopecia areata is associated but is not the most dangerous complication requiring annual laboratory screening.
clinicalPearl: Every patient with vitiligo should have annual TFTs, fasting glucose, and blood pressure checked — the autoimmune polyglandular overlap can be life-threatening if Addison's disease is missed.
keyClue: Generalised vitiligo with autoimmune background requires surveillance for polyglandular autoimmune syndrome components.
commonExamTrap: Selecting only thyroid disease and missing T1DM and Addison's disease — all three are in the core surveillance set.
missedConcept: Vitiligo as a marker of autoimmune polyglandular syndrome requiring structured annual review.

topic: Isotretinoin teratogenicity and pregnancy prevention programme
clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman with severe nodulo-cystic acne has failed two courses of oral antibiotics and topical therapy. Her dermatologist initiates oral isotretinoin. She is sexually active and uses no contraception. Before prescribing, a serum beta-hCG is confirmed negative. Which of the following statements best describes the mandatory pregnancy prevention requirements for this patient during isotretinoin therapy?
options: A single reliable contraceptive method is sufficient if she agrees to monthly pregnancy tests
Two forms of effective contraception must be used simultaneously from 4 weeks before starting until 4 weeks after stopping isotretinoin, with monthly pregnancy monitoring
Contraception is required only for the duration of treatment, as isotretinoin is cleared within 48 hours of the final dose
Condoms alone are sufficient if used correctly, because isotretinoin has a short half-life
Hormonal contraception must be avoided as it worsens isotretinoin-induced dyslipidaemia
explanation: Isotretinoin is a potent teratogen (Category X) causing major foetal abnormalities including craniofacial, cardiac, and CNS malformations in up to 25-30% of exposed pregnancies. Pregnancy Prevention Programmes (PPP) internationally mandate TWO simultaneous effective contraceptive methods from at least 4 weeks before the first dose until at least 4 weeks after the last dose, combined with monthly serum or urine pregnancy testing. This is mandatory and not negotiable.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A single contraceptive method is insufficient under PPP requirements; two simultaneous methods are mandatory due to the catastrophic teratogenic risk.
Correct — this precisely describes the PPP requirement: dual contraception from 4 weeks before to 4 weeks after, with monthly pregnancy tests.

Isotretinoin is cleared within days, but the 4-week post-treatment contraception window is mandatory precisely because ovulation timing relative to the last dose is uncertain; the claim about 48-hour clearance is also inaccurate for the teratogenic risk window.
Condoms alone are classified as an unreliable contraceptive method and cannot be the sole method under PPP; the half-life argument is clinically irrelevant to the regulatory requirement.
Hormonal contraception is actually recommended as one of the two effective methods; it does not meaningfully worsen isotretinoin-related dyslipidaemia at standard doses and must not be withheld.
clinicalPearl: Isotretinoin requires dual contraception from 4 weeks before to 4 weeks after treatment plus monthly pregnancy tests — this is a legal/regulatory requirement, not just a recommendation.
keyClue: Severe nodulo-cystic acne + sexually active woman + isotretinoin initiation = mandatory Pregnancy Prevention Programme.
commonExamTrap: Assuming one reliable contraceptive method (e.g., the pill alone) satisfies requirements — two simultaneous methods are mandatory.
missedConcept: The dual-contraception requirement and the 4-week pre/post-treatment window in PPP for isotretinoin.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man is referred after bone marrow biopsy confirms systemic mastocytosis (SM). He has urticaria pigmentosa lesions, recurrent anaphylaxis, splenomegaly, and a serum tryptase of 142 ng/mL. His blood count shows anaemia (Hb 9.8 g/dL) and thrombocytopenia (platelets 88 × 10⁹/L). Bone marrow shows 40% mast cell infiltration. Which of the following indicates he has aggressive systemic mastocytosis (ASM) and what is the most important prognostic implication?
options: The urticaria pigmentosa skin lesions confirm ASM; prognosis is benign with antihistamine therapy alone
The serum tryptase >20 ng/mL alone establishes ASM; median survival is over 20 years
The presence of C-findings (cytopenias, organomegaly with organ dysfunction, and high marrow infiltration) defines ASM, which carries a median survival of approximately 3-5 years
Recurrent anaphylaxis is a C-finding that alone defines ASM with curative potential using omalizumab
ASM is defined by skin involvement plus any two B-findings; prognosis depends solely on tryptase level
explanation: Aggressive systemic mastocytosis is defined by the presence of C-findings: mast cell-related organ damage including cytopenias (Hb <10 g/dL, platelets <100), liver dysfunction with ascites, malabsorption, skeletal lesions with pathological fractures, or splenomegaly with hypersplenism. This patient has anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and massive marrow infiltration — all C-findings. ASM carries a median survival of approximately 3-5 years and may progress to mast cell leukaemia. B-findings (borderline) do not cause organ damage and define 'smouldering' SM.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urticaria pigmentosa confirms cutaneous mast cell involvement but is not a C-finding and alone does not diagnose ASM; management goes far beyond antihistamines in ASM.
Tryptase >20 ng/mL is a minor diagnostic criterion for SM generally, not specifically ASM; the C-findings, not tryptase alone, define ASM.
Correct — C-findings (cytopenias, organ dysfunction) define ASM and median survival is approximately 3-5 years.
Anaphylaxis is a B-symptom-like feature in SM but is NOT a C-finding; omalizumab may help mast cell mediator symptoms but is not curative for ASM.
This confuses B-findings (no organ damage, smouldering SM) with C-findings (organ damage, ASM); tryptase level alone does not determine prognosis.
clinicalPearl: C-findings (organ damage: cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly with dysfunction, bone involvement) define aggressive systemic mastocytosis and confer a median survival of 3-5 years — these patients need haematology-oncology co-management.
keyClue: Anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and 40% bone marrow mast cell infiltration = C-findings = ASM.
commonExamTrap: Confusing B-findings (borderline, smouldering SM — no organ damage) with C-findings (organ damage, ASM).
missedConcept: The B-finding vs C-finding distinction in mastocytosis staging and its direct impact on prognosis.

topic: Acne scarring — prevention and risk factors
clinicalStem: A 16-year-old boy presents with moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne (multiple papules, pustules, and early nodules) on his face, chest, and back. He has been picking and squeezing his lesions. His father has significant acne scars on his cheeks. He has been using OTC benzoyl peroxide with partial response. Which combination of factors in this patient's history most strongly predicts a high risk of developing permanent atrophic acne scarring?
options: Age of onset in teenage years and male sex only
Partial response to benzoyl peroxide monotherapy and chest involvement
Severe inflammatory or nodulocystic acne, delay in effective treatment, mechanical manipulation of lesions, and family history of scarring
High sebum production and Propionibacterium acnes colonisation density
Dietary factors including high glycaemic index diet and dairy consumption
explanation: The strongest evidence-based predictors of permanent atrophic scarring in acne are: severity of inflammatory disease (especially nodular/cystic), delay in initiating effective treatment, persistent physical manipulation (picking/squeezing — this directly extends inflammation), and a positive family history of scarring (genetic predisposition to abnormal wound healing). This patient has all four risk factors, making early escalation to systemic therapy (oral isotretinoin) the priority.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Male sex and teenage onset are epidemiological factors but are not the strongest predictors of scarring risk independently.
Partial benzoyl peroxide response and chest involvement indicate undertreated disease but are not the core scarring predictors in the validated literature.
Correct — all four listed factors are validated predictors of permanent atrophic acne scarring.
Sebum and bacterial colonisation contribute to acne severity but are not independently validated as scarring predictors; scarring risk is tied to inflammatory depth and duration.
Dietary factors influence acne activity in some patients but have minimal evidence as independent predictors of scar formation.
clinicalPearl: Early aggressive treatment (consider isotretinoin) in patients with severe/nodular acne, manipulation habits, and a family history of scarring can prevent irreversible atrophic scars.
keyClue: Nodular acne + lesion picking + positive family history of scars = high scarring risk requiring prompt escalation.
commonExamTrap: Attributing scarring risk primarily to diet or bacterial factors while ignoring the mechanical trauma from picking and treatment delay.
missedConcept: Family history and mechanical manipulation as modifiable/predictive scarring risk factors alongside disease severity.

clinicalStem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a palpable purpuric rash on his buttocks and lower limbs, arthralgia of his ankles and knees, colicky abdominal pain, and microscopic haematuria on urine dipstick. His platelet count is 310 × 10⁹/L and coagulation screen is normal. A clinical diagnosis of Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) is made. His rash and joint symptoms resolve over 4 weeks. At his 3-month follow-up he remains asymptomatic. Which of the following monitoring plans is most appropriate going forward?
options: Discharge at 3 months if the rash has not recurred — no further follow-up needed
Annual abdominal ultrasound for 5 years to detect bowel complications
Urine dipstick and blood pressure monitoring at 3, 6, and 12 months, and then annually for at least 1-2 years, with nephrology referral if haematuria or proteinuria persists
Serum IgA levels every 6 months — elevated levels predict renal failure
Daily urine dipstick by parents at home for the remainder of childhood
explanation: IgA vasculitis (HSP) carries a risk of IgA nephropathy that may lead to chronic renal disease, nephrotic syndrome, or renal failure in 1-2% of cases. Renal involvement may be subclinical initially. Guidelines recommend urine dipstick for protein/blood and blood pressure check at 3, 6, and 12 months, then annually for 1-2 years minimum (some guidelines extend to 2 years). Nephrology referral is warranted if persistent proteinuria or rising creatinine is detected. An initially asymptomatic patient can still develop significant nephropathy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Discharging at 3 months is inappropriate — renal involvement can present weeks to months after the rash resolves and requires structured surveillance.
Abdominal ultrasound surveillance is not part of standard HSP follow-up; bowel complications (intussusception) are an acute not chronic complication.
Correct — structured urine and BP monitoring for 1-2 years is the recommended follow-up, with nephrology referral for persistent abnormalities.
Serum IgA is frequently elevated in HSP but does not reliably predict renal outcome and is not a recommended follow-up test.
Daily home dipstick is impractical, unvalidated, and not recommended in guidelines — structured clinic-based monitoring is standard.
clinicalPearl: In IgA vasculitis (HSP), renal involvement can develop or worsen weeks after the rash resolves — urine dipstick and BP monitoring for at least 1-2 years post-diagnosis is mandatory even if the child appears well.
keyClue: Initial microscopic haematuria + IgA vasculitis = obligatory renal surveillance even after clinical resolution.
commonExamTrap: Discharging the patient once the rash resolves — renal complications can emerge or worsen silently during recovery.
missedConcept: The risk of progressive IgA nephropathy in HSP requiring long-term post-resolution renal monitoring.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old South Asian woman presents with dark brown patches on her cheeks, temples, and upper lip that appeared after a course of acne treatment. Her acne has since resolved. She is distressed by the pigmentation. Examination confirms flat, non-scaly, macular hyperpigmentation in a distribution overlying the previous acne lesions. Wood's lamp accentuates the pigmentation. She asks how long it will take to resolve and what she should avoid. Which of the following statements about her post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) is most accurate?
options: PIH in Fitzpatrick skin types IV-VI is epidermal and typically resolves completely within 4-6 weeks without treatment
PIH that accentuates under Wood's lamp is dermal in location and rarely resolves spontaneously
Epidermal PIH (Wood's lamp accentuated) can resolve over months with sun protection and topical hypopigmenting agents; UV exposure is the most important factor worsening and prolonging PIH
PIH in dark skin types is always dermal and requires laser treatment for resolution
Wood's lamp accentuation of pigment confirms melanoma in situ, requiring urgent biopsy
explanation: Wood's lamp accentuation of pigment indicates the pigment is epidermal (melanin in the epidermis absorbs UV and fluoresces brighter). Epidermal PIH has a better prognosis than dermal PIH and can resolve over weeks to months with strict sun protection and topical agents (hydroquinone, azelaic acid, niacinamide). UV exposure is the principal factor that darkens and prolongs PIH, making broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen the cornerstone of management regardless of other treatments.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Epidermal PIH in darker skin types does NOT resolve in 4-6 weeks; it requires months to years and consistent sun protection — untreated, it can persist indefinitely.
Dermal PIH (Wood's lamp does NOT accentuate) is the variant with poorer prognosis; Wood's lamp accentuation indicates epidermal, not dermal, involvement.
Correct — epidermal PIH (Wood's lamp positive) can improve significantly with sun protection and topical hypopigmenting agents; UV is the key aggravating factor.
PIH in darker skin types is frequently epidermal (though dermal involvement can coexist); laser is not always required and is often the last resort given the risk of further PIH.
Wood's lamp accentuation of PIH is a benign clinical sign of epidermal pigment — it has no association with melanoma in situ.
clinicalPearl: Wood's lamp accentuates epidermal melanin (better prognosis) but not dermal melanin (poor prognosis) — always use it to classify PIH depth before counselling on timeline.
keyClue: Wood's lamp accentuation = epidermal melanin = better prognosis with sun protection.
commonExamTrap: Confusing Wood's lamp accentuation (epidermal, better prognosis) with no accentuation (dermal, worse prognosis).
missedConcept: The Wood's lamp test as a clinical tool to determine PIH depth and thus predict resolution potential.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman of Afro-Caribbean descent presents with a longitudinal brown-black streak (melanonychia longitudinalis) on her right thumbnail that has been present for 18 months. She was previously reassured by her GP that it was benign ethnic pigmentation. On current examination, the streak is 5 mm wide, the proximal nail fold shows a brownish discolouration (Hutchinson's sign), and the nail plate is partially dystrophic. Dermoscopy shows irregular pigmentation with grey lines. Which of the following statements best describes the prognostic implications of her presentation?
options: Hutchinson's sign is a normal ethnic variant in Afro-Caribbean patients and does not change prognosis
The 18-month delay in referral is clinically irrelevant as subungual melanoma is always detected at early Breslow depth

Hutchinson's sign, nail dystrophy, and irregular dermoscopic pigmentation are high-risk features for subungual melanoma; delayed diagnosis is associated with significantly worse prognosis due to deeper Breslow thickness at the time of excision
A 5 mm streak width is below the threshold of concern; referral is only required if the streak exceeds 8 mm
Dermoscopic grey lines are reassuring and indicate benign lentigines rather than melanoma
explanation: Subungual melanoma (SUM) frequently presents as melanonychia longitudinalis and carries a poor prognosis compared to cutaneous melanoma, largely because it is diagnosed late. Hutchinson's sign (pigment extension onto the proximal nail fold) is a highly suspicious clinical sign for SUM. Delayed diagnosis allows progression to deeper Breslow thickness, with 5-year survival dropping from >90% (thin, <1 mm) to <50% (thick, >4 mm). Grey irregular dermoscopic lines and nail dystrophy further elevate suspicion. Urgent dermatology referral is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hutchinson's sign is NOT a normal ethnic variant — pseudo-Hutchinson's sign (pigment visible through the translucent nail fold but not actually in the fold skin) can occur in darker skin types, but true Hutchinson's sign (pigment in the fold skin itself) is a red-flag feature regardless of ethnicity.
Subungual melanoma is often diagnosed at advanced Breslow depth precisely because of reassurance and delayed referral — this claim is dangerously false.
Correct — Hutchinson's sign, dystrophy, wide streak, and irregular dermoscopy are all high-risk features; 18-month delay significantly worsens Breslow-depth-dependent prognosis.
There is no validated 8 mm rule; any widening, irregularity, or Hutchinson's sign warrants urgent referral regardless of absolute width.
Grey irregular lines on nail dermoscopy are associated with melanoma; regular parallel lines in a grey-brown background are more reassuring — irregular grey lines are suspicious.
clinicalPearl: True Hutchinson's sign (periungual pigmentation) in melanonychia is a red flag for subungual melanoma requiring urgent dermatology referral — delayed diagnosis leads to deeper Breslow thickness and far worse survival.
keyClue: Hutchinson's sign + widening melanonychia + nail dystrophy + irregular dermoscopy = urgent subungual melanoma referral.
commonExamTrap: Attributing Hutchinson's sign to ethnic pigmentation variant in dark-skinned patients — true Hutchinson's sign is never a normal finding in any ethnicity.
missedConcept: The distinction between true and pseudo-Hutchinson's sign and the implications of delayed subungual melanoma diagnosis on Breslow-depth-dependent prognosis.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old male who was started on oral isotretinoin 6 weeks ago returns urgently. Within 2 weeks of starting treatment his acne dramatically worsened, with multiple haemorrhagic and necrotic nodules and plaques erupting on his trunk and face. He now has fever (38.8°C), bone pain in his sternum and tibiae, malaise, and weight loss. Blood tests: WBC 18 × 10⁹/L (neutrophilia), CRP 98 mg/L, ESR 82 mm/hr. X-ray of his sternum shows osteolytic lesions. Which of the following best explains his current condition and the most appropriate immediate management?
options: He has developed a staphylococcal skin infection from disrupted acne lesions; start IV flucloxacillin and continue isotretinoin
This is an expected isotretinoin flare; increase the isotretinoin dose to suppress sebum more rapidly

He has acne fulminans — a rare, severe systemic complication; immediate management includes stopping isotretinoin and starting systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone), with isotretinoin reintroduced later at low dose under steroid cover
He has developed drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS) from isotretinoin; stop isotretinoin permanently
Osteolytic lesions in acne suggest an underlying bone malignancy unrelated to isotretinoin; refer to orthopaedics urgently
explanation: Acne fulminans (AF) is a rare, severe inflammatory complication characterised by sudden-onset haemorrhagic and ulcerative acne with systemic features: fever, arthralgia/bone pain, elevated inflammatory markers, and osteolytic bone lesions (especially sternal and tibial). It can be triggered or unmasked by isotretinoin initiation. Management requires immediate cessation of isotretinoin and initiation of systemic prednisolone to control the inflammatory storm; isotretinoin is reintroduced 4-6 weeks later at low dose (0.1-0.2 mg/kg/day) under steroid cover, with slow dose escalation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The osteolytic bone lesions, systemic inflammation, and haemorrhagic acne pattern are not consistent with isolated staphylococcal skin infection; continuing isotretinoin would worsen AF.
Increasing the isotretinoin dose is contraindicated in acne fulminans — it may have triggered this complication and must be stopped immediately.
Correct — acne fulminans requires isotretinoin cessation, systemic corticosteroids, and later careful isotretinoin reintroduction.
DRESS presents with morbilliform rash, facial oedema, lymphadenopathy, and characteristic eosinophilia — not haemorrhagic acne nodules and osteolytic lesions; this is acne fulminans.
Osteolytic lesions in acne fulminans are a recognised systemic feature of the condition, not primary bone malignancy — clinical context makes this clear.
clinicalPearl: Acne fulminans is a systemic emergency — it presents with haemorrhagic/ulcerative acne, fever, arthralgia, and osteolytic bone lesions, often triggered by isotretinoin; stop the drug, start steroids immediately.
keyClue: Isotretinoin initiation → rapid flare of haemorrhagic acne + fever + bone pain + osteolytic lesions = acne fulminans.
commonExamTrap: Increasing the isotretinoin dose for a perceived 'initial flare' when the clinical picture is acne fulminans — a dangerous error.
missedConcept: Acne fulminans as a systemic inflammatory complication triggered by isotretinoin requiring steroid therapy, not dose escalation.
redFlagReason: Acne fulminans is a systemic emergency with fever, bone destruction, and severe skin necrosis; continuing or increasing isotretinoin without steroid cover can result in life-threatening sepsis, pathological fractures, and severe systemic morbidity.

topic: Chronic spontaneous urticaria — follow-up and disease course
clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with daily wheals and flares that have been present for 8 weeks. The wheals are pruritic, migratory, resolve within 24 hours without leaving marks, and are not triggered by any identifiable allergen, physical stimulus, or NSAIDs. Blood tests: FBC, CRP, TFTs, and anti-TPO antibodies are normal. She is diagnosed with chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) and started on a non-sedating antihistamine. She asks about the long-term outlook. Which of the following statements most accurately describes the natural history of CSU?
options: CSU is almost always lifelong once established; >80% of patients have symptoms for over 10 years
CSU spontaneously remits within 3 months in the majority of patients with normal anti-TPO antibodies
Approximately 50% of CSU patients achieve remission within 1 year; however, 20% continue to have disease at 5 years, and a subset has a protracted course correlated with autoimmune markers
CSU always progresses to anaphylaxis if untreated; she requires an adrenaline auto-injector
CSU resolves permanently after a single 3-month course of antihistamines in most patients
explanation: The natural history of CSU is variable but generally favourable for many patients. Population studies show that approximately 50% of patients achieve remission within 1 year, but about 20% have ongoing disease at 5 years. Patients with elevated anti-TPO antibodies (Hashimoto's-associated CSU) and autologous serum skin test positivity tend to have more prolonged disease. CSU does not intrinsically carry an anaphylaxis risk — it is a mast cell-driven non-IgE urticaria without an allergen-specific anaphylactic mechanism.
wrongAnswerExplanations: >80% lifetime chronicity is an overestimate; roughly half of patients remit within 1 year and many more over time, though a subset does have prolonged disease.
Normal anti-TPO does not guarantee rapid spontaneous remission within 3 months; remission rates at 3 months are modest, and 50% remission is the approximate 1-year figure.
Correct — approximately 50% remit by 1 year; 20% persist at 5 years; autoimmune markers correlate with longer disease.
CSU does not cause anaphylaxis; the wheals represent cutaneous mast cell mediator release without systemic IgE-mediated allergen sensitisation — an auto-injector is not routinely indicated.
Antihistamines suppress symptoms but are not disease-modifying; disease remits based on the underlying immunological course, not antihistamine duration.
clinicalPearl: CSU remits in about half of patients within 1 year, but 20% still have disease at 5 years — counsel patients for potentially prolonged treatment and monitor for autoimmune thyroid disease.
keyClue: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (>6 weeks, no trigger identified) — natural history data for patient counselling.
commonExamTrap: Equating CSU with allergic urticaria and recommending anaphylaxis precautions — CSU is not IgE-mediated and does not confer anaphylaxis risk.
missedConcept: The variable but generally self-limiting natural history of CSU and the role of autoimmune markers as prognostic indicators.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman on the combined oral contraceptive pill achieved excellent lightening of her facial melasma after 12 weeks of triple combination cream (hydroquinone 4% + tretinoin 0.05% + fluocinolone acetonide 0.01%) combined with strict photoprotection (SPF 50+ daily). She asks whether she can now stop the cream and the sunscreen, as her skin looks normal. Which of the following represents the most evidence-based advice regarding her ongoing management?
options: She can stop all treatment now that her melasma has cleared; melasma does not relapse once treated
She should continue the triple combination cream indefinitely without interruption to prevent any future relapse

She should switch to a maintenance regimen (sunscreen SPF 50+ daily, with or without topical azelaic acid or low-concentration hydroquinone), switch to a non-oestrogen-containing contraceptive if feasible, and understand that relapse is highly likely with UV exposure or continued oestrogen stimulus
She only needs to reapply the triple combination cream in summer months; no sunscreen is required in winter
A single session of Q-switched laser will permanently eliminate her melasma without need for further photoprotection
explanation: Melasma is a chronic, relapsing condition driven by UV exposure, hormonal factors (oestrogens, progesterone), and genetic predisposition. Clearance with treatment does not mean cure — relapse is the rule without ongoing photoprotection and hormonal trigger management. Triple combination cream should be used in limited cycles (avoid >6 months continuous use due to risks of ochronosis and skin atrophy from corticosteroid component), then transitioned to maintenance with SPF 50+, azelaic acid, niacinamide, or low-dose hydroquinone. Switching the COCP to a progestogen-only or non-hormonal method can significantly reduce recurrence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Melasma commonly relapses, especially with continued UV exposure and oestrogen stimulus — cessation of treatment without maintenance or trigger modification is associated with near-universal relapse.

Indefinite continuous use of triple combination cream is not recommended due to the risk of cutaneous atrophy (steroid component), ochronosis (hydroquinone), and retinoid irritation — cyclic use with maintenance is preferred.
Correct — maintenance photoprotection, consideration of COCP switch, and patient education about relapse risk are the evidence-based recommendations.
UV exposure occurs year-round including cloud and indoor settings; limiting sunscreen to summer only is inadequate and scientifically unsound.
Laser can worsen melasma by triggering PIH, especially in darker skin types, and does not eliminate the underlying tendency to relapse with UV/hormone exposure.
clinicalPearl: Melasma clears but does not cure — maintenance SPF 50+ plus removal of hormonal triggers (switch COCP) is essential to prevent almost-certain relapse.
keyClue: Combined OCP use + UV exposure = dual active melasma triggers; clearance without trigger modification predicts rapid relapse.
commonExamTrap: Recommending indefinite triple combination cream use — the corticosteroid component causes cumulative atrophy and ochronosis with prolonged use.
missedConcept: Melasma as a chronic relapsing condition requiring maintenance therapy and trigger modification rather than a curable acute process.
